All reports
NEXQLOUD SEALED

What’s Next Proposal

The defined work that turns the proof artifact from a moat opportunity into a proven moat.

Prepared by Fenn and Hart
June 2026

0. The Commitment

Bottom line. The next phase produces the one asset that turns Sealed’s strongest claim from a moat opportunity into a proven moat: a regulated design-partner reference where an auditor or regulator accepts the forwarded per-action receipt for a real closed-model call, captured as a named case study. Around that proof, the phase rebuilds the buyer-facing surface so the evidence is reachable, separates Sealed’s own credentials from the parent’s, and arms the demand campaign and the dual-buyer play. The production reference comes first. Every other move gets stronger once it lands.

This phase operationalizes the central choice the Strategic Alignment Brief settled: lead on the proof artifact, the regulator-forwardable per-action receipt no named rival can match. The Brief named the test that moves that claim from leans to proven, a production regulated-cohort reference where a regulator accepts the forwarded audit trail governing a real closed-model call. This phase runs that test and builds the surfaces, credentials, and campaigns that carry the result to the buyer. Targeting in this proposal uses the same dual buyer set the Brief and the Marketing and Messaging Guide define: the regulated organization that owns the data and the liability, and the AI vendor stalled at security review.

1. Initiative Cards

Six initiatives carry the phase. Each maps to one next-phase action the Brief left open for scoping. The first proves the moat. The rest make the proof visible, checkable, and sellable.

Initiative 1. The production-proof and design-partner evidence program

  1. Confidence this is the right next move. 5/5. This is the conviction axis, how sure we are it is the right move, separate from the Brief’s evidence-confidence on the claim itself. The Brief rates the proof-artifact spine as leans, and names exactly one test that settles it. Producing that proof is the single highest-leverage thing the company can do, and nothing else in this phase reaches full strength without it.
  2. The Move. We convert one regulated design partner into a named case study in which an auditor or regulator accepts the per-action receipt forwarded without contacting the company, governing a real closed-model call.
  3. Why Now. The Brief’s spine rests on a claim the company cannot yet prove: that a regulator will accept the forwarded receipt across the closed-model boundary. The company is at design-partner stage with no production cohort, so the moat stays an opportunity until one reference clears the regulator-acceptance test. The ledger also carries no first-party customer interviews, so the buyer evidence behind the whole position is thinner than the position deserves.
  4. Scope. Select two or three design-partner accounts in healthcare or financial services that already run a governed closed-model workflow. Instrument one production call so the forwarded receipt reaches the partner’s auditor or examiner. Capture the regulator-acceptance moment, the named partner, and the verifiable outcome as an attributable case study, with an anonymized-but-verifiable fallback if no partner grants attribution. Run AI-led custom interviews across the same accounts and the stalled-AI-vendor buyer to harden the voice-of-customer evidence and confirm which forcing event drove the buy.

Effort. 4/5.

Sequence note. This is the do-first initiative. It needs a design partner who will instrument a real call and permit attribution. Until it lands, the proof-artifact headline ships as the moat opportunity, and Initiatives 5 and 6 carry a claim the production reference is built to settle. Start it before anything else in this phase.

Initiative 2. The Sealed buyer-facing surface and live verifier

  1. The Move. We stand up a buyer-facing surface under the “NexQloud Sealed” identity that carries the governance story and links the live verifier where a regulated buyer running diligence will reach it.
  2. Why Now. Today Sealed’s best evidence sits off the surface a buyer touches. The governance story and the live verifier run under the NexArt brand, while nexqloud.io sells decentralized-cloud cost savings, so the proof is invisible at the moment it is meant to close the deal. A buyer who searches “NexQloud Sealed” finds almost no public footprint. The verifier is live and the story is written. Both sit on a surface the buyer never reaches.
  3. Scope. Build the Sealed surface, migrate the disciplined governance narrative onto it, and embed a working link to the no-account verifier so a security reviewer can check a receipt without contacting the company. Carry the same per-action-receipt headline the Brief resolves. Retire the proof-theater conventions on the way in: the zeroed vanity counters, the press-logo wall, and the round-number testimonials have no place on a surface whose credibility rests on evidence over adjectives.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 5/5, distinct from evidence-confidence. The Brief rates this gap as a settled, directly-observed fact about the company’s own surfaces, so the conviction here is as high as the move itself is plain.

Effort. 3/5.

Sequence note. Depends on the credential split in Initiative 3 being ready to embed, and gains its strongest proof point once Initiative 1 produces the case study to feature. Stand up the surface in parallel with Initiative 3; feature the reference when it lands.

Initiative 3. The credential separation page

  1. Why Now. A sophisticated buyer running a security review will compare what the company implies against what it can verify, and the current proof stack blurs the line. The only credentials a buyer can check today, the announced patents and the SOC 2 Type 1, belong to the parent DCP. Letting the parent’s proofs stand in for Sealed’s invites the exact diligence contradiction that kills credibility with the careful buyer the company most wants.
  2. The Move. We publish a page that lays the parent-company credentials and the Sealed-specific credentials side by side, each marked filed, in progress, or held, so a buyer can tell which credential belongs to which product.
  3. Scope. One page, two columns. The parent column carries the announced DCP Cloud-OS patents and the DCP SOC 2 Type 1 as context. The Sealed column carries the Sealed-specific patents marked filed, the Sealed SOC 2 Type II marked in progress, and the live verifier marked checkable now. State the honest status of each item, so the buyer reads a stack with no implied finish line [client to confirm before publication].
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5, the conviction axis. The Brief settles the attribution gap on direct observation of the live surfaces, and conceding the gaps in the open is the move that protects diligence credibility, so conviction is high even though the honest version concedes work still underway.

Effort. 2/5.

Sequence note. Depends on a clean internal inventory of which patents and certifications are Sealed’s versus the parent’s. Feeds the Sealed surface in Initiative 2 directly. Build it first within the surface workstream.

Initiative 4. Complete and surface the Sealed-specific proofs

  1. Effort. 4/5. This is the heaviest lift in the phase, because it runs on outside clocks: patent prosecution and an audit cycle the company does not fully control.
  2. The Move. We sequence the Sealed patent prosecution, the Sealed SOC 2 Type II, and the verifier onto a Sealed-owned surface so each becomes independently checkable in Sealed’s own name.
  3. Why Now. The credential separation page in Initiative 3 tells a buyer the truth about today’s gaps. This initiative closes them. The Sealed patents are filed and await grant. The Sealed SOC 2 Type II is still in audit. The verifier runs today under the NexArt brand. Each of these is the work that turns a conceded gap into a checkable credential, and the structural moat the Brief describes stays unproven in name until they land [client to confirm before publication].
  4. Scope. Drive the five Sealed-specific patents through prosecution toward grant. Complete the Sealed SOC 2 Type II and publish the report reference in Sealed’s own name. Reattach the verifier to a Sealed-owned domain so a buyer reaches it without leaving the Sealed surface. Update the credential separation page as each item moves from filed to granted and from in progress to complete.
  5. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5, the conviction axis, separate from the Brief’s evidence-confidence. These are the credentials a regulated buyer checks first, so completing them is clearly right. The score sits at 4 because the timing runs on external prosecution and audit cycles the company does not fully control.
  6. Sequence note. Runs longest of any initiative because it depends on external patent and audit timelines. Start the audit and prosecution work early and let it run in the background while the faster surface and demand work ships ahead of it.

Initiative 5. The multi-trigger regulatory demand campaign

  1. The Move. We build the demand campaign on a basket of active, non-slipping US federal and sectoral forcing events, mapped to the healthcare and financial-services beachhead, and equip sales to match the right trigger to each vertical.
  2. Why Now. A pitch built on one fixed deadline gets falsified when the deadline moves, and the clean single dates keep moving: the EU high-risk obligations slipped to December 2027, Colorado’s AI Act to January 2027, and NYDFS added no new AI rules. The durable triggers are the active ones that hold: Reg S-P smaller-entity compliance with its 30-day customer-notification window, CMMC Phase 2 third-party certification, the NAIC AI Model Bulletin now adopted across 24-plus states, the pending HIPAA Security Rule update, and the first shadow-AI SEC 8-K already filed.
  3. Scope. Map each active trigger to the vertical it forces. Build a per-vertical proof and battle-card kit that pairs the trigger with the receipt evidence and answers the security-review questions a buyer brings. Equip sales to lead with the one trigger that applies to the account in front of them. Anchor no campaign on a single fixed external date.
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5, the conviction axis, distinct from the Brief’s evidence-confidence. The Brief rates the demand framing as leans, since the basket-versus-single-deadline posture is a leadership call the evidence favors but does not close. The conviction that the basket is the durable frame runs high: every clean single date that could anchor a campaign has already slipped.

Effort. 3/5.

Sequence note. Depends on leadership ratifying the basket posture over a single headline deadline, and sharpens once Initiative 1 supplies a named reference to attach to the trigger. Launch after the posture is ratified.

Initiative 6. The dual-buyer channel play

  1. The Move. We run one go-to-market play that leads both the regulated organization and the stalled AI vendor with the same story: forwardable proof gets you through the security review.
  2. Why Now. Both buyers live the same security-review moment, and the Brief treats them as two facets of one buyer set. The regulated contractor vets every vendor it shares data with. The AI vendor stalls at the review because it cannot show what its agent does with protected data. Customers themselves warn that the badge alone carries no value, so the wedge into both has to be the proof. The market already treats a SOC 2 checkbox as table stakes.
  3. Scope. Lead both buyers with the forwardable-proof story and position SOC 2 as table stakes the receipt transcends. Run the faster AI-vendor motion to win the early references, then use those wins to open the enterprise regulated accounts they unlock. Build the talk-track and objection map that carry one wedge into two buyer conversations [client to confirm before publication].
  4. Confidence this is the right next move. 4/5, the conviction axis, separate from the Brief’s evidence-confidence. The Brief rates the dual-ICP wedge as leans, since the strongest signal that a checkbox alone carries no value began single-sourced before independent corroboration firmed it. Conviction that one wedge serves both buyers runs high. The score sits at 4 because delivery still depends on the proof reality maturing under Initiative 1.

Effort. 3/5.

Sequence note. Depends most heavily on Initiative 1, because the dual-buyer story leans on a proof claim the production reference is built to settle. Hold the enterprise push until the case study exists. Run the lighter AI-vendor outreach in parallel to seed the first references.

2. Sequencing

The phase orders by dependency, and one initiative gates the rest.

Initiative 1 leads, because it produces the proof every other move leans on. The production reference moves the spine from leans to proven, and it is the one asset Initiatives 5 and 6 need to sell the headline claim without a hedge.

The surface workstream runs next and in parallel within itself. Initiative 3, the credential split, feeds Initiative 2, the Sealed surface, so build the split first and stand up the surface around it. Both ship ahead of the production reference and feature it once it lands.

Initiative 4 runs longest and quietly. Start the patent prosecution and the SOC 2 Type II audit early and let them run on their own external clocks while the faster work ships, then surface each credential the moment it clears.

Initiatives 5 and 6 launch last, because they sell the proven claim outward. Initiative 5 starts once leadership ratifies the basket posture. Initiative 6 runs the light AI-vendor outreach in parallel to seed early references, and holds the enterprise push until the Initiative 1 case study exists to carry it.

3. What We Need From You

Each initiative needs a decision or an input from leadership before it can start.

  • For Initiative 1, the production-proof program. Name the design-partner accounts to approach, authorize instrumenting one production call so the receipt reaches the partner’s auditor, and decide the attribution posture: named case study preferred, anonymized-but-verifiable as the fallback if no partner will be named.
  • For Initiative 2, the Sealed surface. Approve standing up a buyer-facing surface under the “NexQloud Sealed” identity, and confirm the decision to retire the vanity counters, the press-logo wall, and the round-number testimonials.
  • For Initiative 3, the credential split. Supply the internal inventory of which patents and certifications belong to Sealed versus the parent DCP, and confirm the honest status of each for publication [client to confirm before publication].
  • For Initiative 4, completing the proofs. Commit the budget and the owners for the patent prosecution and the Sealed SOC 2 Type II, and authorize reattaching the verifier to a Sealed-owned domain.
  • For Initiative 5, the demand campaign. Ratify the multi-trigger basket as the demand posture over a single headline deadline, the leadership choice the Brief left open, and confirm the healthcare and financial-services beachhead order.
  • For Initiative 6, the dual-buyer play. Decide to lead both buyers with forwardable proof and treat SOC 2 as table stakes, and approve running the AI-vendor motion ahead of the enterprise push to build the early references.
